name: light
description: Short factual follow-ups expecting brief 1-2 sentence answers. Designed to fill context slowly.
questions: Give an overview of the major themes, narrative structure, and key literary devices in this work.
Which character most clearly embodies the central theme, and why?
Name one symbol from the work and briefly explain its significance.
What is the narrative point of view, and how does it affect the reader?
Identify one key scene that best captures the author's main message.
How does the title relate to the work's central theme?
What genre conventions does this work follow or subvert?
Name one literary device from the opening pages and describe its effect.
What is the setting, and how does it influence the mood?
How does the work end, and what does the ending suggest about its themes?

name: medium
description: Standard literary analysis questions expecting 1-2 paragraph answers. Moderate context pressure.
questions: Give an overview of the major themes, narrative structure, and key literary devices in this work.
How does the author use imagery to reinforce the central theme?
Trace how the protagonist's perspective shifts across the narrative.
What structural choices does the author make, and how do they serve the work's purpose?
How does the author establish and develop the central conflict?
Analyze the relationship between two contrasting characters and what it reveals thematically.
How does the author's use of language reflect the historical or cultural context?
What role does setting play in shaping character and theme?
How does the author use foreshadowing, and what effect does it have on the reader?
What is the work's attitude toward its subject matter, and how is that attitude communicated?
How do secondary characters contribute to the work's main argument?
What is the significance of the work's resolution, and does it feel earned given what came before?

name: heavy
description: Dense analytical questions expecting multi-paragraph responses. High context pressure — trimming expected on later turns.
questions: Give an overview of the major themes, narrative structure, and key literary devices in this work.
Analyze how the author constructs meaning through the relationship between form and content — does the narrative structure mirror or resist the thematic argument?
How does the author's choice of narrative perspective shape the reader's moral judgment of characters and events, and where does that positioning become unstable or contradictory?
Trace the development of a central motif through three key moments in the text and argue for its thematic significance.
To what extent does the work challenge or reinforce the dominant ideologies of its historical context? Use specific textual evidence to support your argument.
Analyze the author's use of language at the sentence level in a pivotal scene — how do word choice, syntax, and rhythm contribute to meaning?
How does the work position the reader in relation to power? Identify the mechanisms through which the text distributes sympathy and judgment.
Compare the function of two opposing spaces or environments in the work, and argue what their contrast reveals about the work's thematic concerns.
In what ways does the author's biographical or cultural context illuminate aspects of the text that might otherwise remain opaque? Where does this approach risk over-determining interpretation?
Identify a moment of ambiguity or tension in the text — a scene, a line, or a character choice — and argue for two plausible but contradictory readings.
How does the work's use of time — chronology, flashback, compression, dilation — serve its narrative and thematic purposes?
Evaluate the effectiveness of the work's ending. Does it resolve, complicate, or subvert the expectations established by the rest of the text?
What is the work's central argument about human experience, and how successfully does it make that argument? Consider structure, characterisation, language, and form in your response.
Considering everything discussed, articulate the work's most important contribution to literature and explain why it deserves continued study.

name: heavy_2
description: Dense analytical questions expecting multi-paragraph responses with explicit callbacks to earlier answers. High context pressure — trimming expected on later turns.
questions: Give an overview of the major themes, narrative structure, and key literary devices in this work.
Which of those themes do you consider the most central, and what specific textual moments make that case most strongly?
How does the narrative structure you described reinforce that central theme — does the form enact the content in any way?
Zoom in on the opening of the work. What does the author establish in the first pages that prepares the reader for everything you've described?
How does the author use perspective and point of view to control what the reader knows and when — and what does that withholding do thematically?
Trace how the central conflict develops across the work's structure. Where is tension highest, and is the resolution satisfying or deliberately unsatisfying?
Pick the single most important image or symbol in the work and analyze how it accumulates meaning across the text.
How does the author's use of language — syntax, diction, tone — shift across the work, and what do those shifts signal about character or theme?

Returning to the major themes you outlined at the start — now that we've examined structure, perspective, conflict, and language in detail, does your sense of which theme is most central hold, or would you revise it?
Consider the secondary characters. How do they function in relation to the protagonist — as foils, mirrors, or something else entirely?
Where does the author's argument about the world feel most explicit, and where is it most effectively implied rather than stated?
How does the ending recontextualize everything that came before? Does the final image or moment change how you read the opening?
Considering everything we've discussed — structure, perspective, language, symbolism — what is the single greatest strength of this work as a piece of literature, and its greatest limitation?
If you were writing an IB Paper 2 essay on this work, what would your thesis be, and which two or three moments would you build the essay around?

Returning to the narrative structure and literary devices you identified at the very beginning — having now traced them through conflict, character, language, and theme — what does this work argue about the human condition that those specific formal choices make possible, and that no other form could achieve?

name: basic
description: Simple factual questions followed by questions that explicitly reference the answer to Q1, designed to test whether early context is retained or trimmed.
questions: Who is the author of this work, and what are the most essential facts about their life and historical context?
When was this work first published, and what was happening in the world at that time?
Who is the protagonist, and what is their situation at the opening of the work?
What is the setting — time, place, and social environment?
What is the central conflict, stated as simply as possible?
Who are the two or three most important secondary characters?
What is the work's genre, and does it fit that genre comfortably or uneasily?
What is the most memorable scene in the work, and why does it stand out?
What is the work's ending, described factually?
What is the author's general style — how would you describe the prose or verse to someone who has never read it?
Returning to the author you named at the very start — what other works did they write, and how does this one fit into their broader career?
Still thinking about that author — what personal experiences or beliefs of theirs are most visible in this work?
Going back to what you said about the author at the beginning — how did their historical moment shape the choices they made in writing this work?
Returning to the author's life and context you described first — if they were writing this work today, what would they change and what would they keep?
You described the author at the start of our conversation — based on everything we've now discussed about the work itself, do you think the author achieved what they set out to do?
Thinking again about who this author is and where they came from — what is the single most important thing about their identity or context that a reader must understand to fully appreciate this work?
Returning one more time to your very first answer about the author — has anything we've discussed changed how you would describe them now, or does your original characterisation still hold?
Finally, and with the author you named at the start firmly in mind — what does this work tell us about them as a human being, beyond what any biography could say?
